mcqs.question: What is the primary goal of a page replacement algorithm in virtual memory systems?

options.text: To maximize CPU utilization
To minimize the page fault rate
To maximize the throughput of the system
To minimize the memory access time

mcqs.explanation: The primary goal of page replacement algorithms is to minimize the page fault rate. When a page fault occurs and no free frames are available, the algorithm selects a victim page to evict. A good algorithm should evict pages that will not be needed again soon, thereby reducing future page faults.

mcqs.question: In the FIFO page replacement algorithm, which page is selected for eviction when a page fault occurs?

options.text: The page that was most recently used
The page that has been in memory the longest
The page with the oldest modification time
The page that is most frequently accessed

mcqs.explanation: FIFO (First-In-First-Out) selects the page that has been in memory the longest for eviction. It treats memory frames like a queue - the first page brought into memory is the first one to be replaced.

mcqs.question: Consider a system with 3 frames and the reference string: 1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. How many page faults occur using FIFO?
mcqs.explanation: Using FIFO with 3 frames: Frames start empty. Pages 1,2,3 cause 3 faults. Page 4 replaces 1 (fault 4). Page 1 replaces 2 (fault 5). Page 2 replaces 3 (fault 6). Page 5 replaces 4 (fault 7). Page 1 is in memory. Page 2 is in memory. Page 3 replaces 5 (fault 8). Page 4 replaces 1 (fault 9). Page 5 replaces 2 (fault 10). Then 5 is already there. Total = 11 faults.

mcqs.question: What is Belady's Anomaly?

options.text: An increase in page faults when increasing the number of frames
A decrease in page faults when decreasing the number of frames
A situation where optimal page replacement is impossible to implement
A type of page fault that cannot be handled by the OS

mcqs.explanation: Belady's Anomaly (also called FIFO anomaly) is the counterintuitive phenomenon where increasing the number of frames can lead to an increase in page faults. This occurs because FIFO does not consider the pages' usage pattern and may evict a page that is still being actively used.

mcqs.question: Which of the following page replacement algorithms can suffer from Belady's Anomaly?

options.text: Optimal (OPT)
Least Recently Used (LRU)
FIFO
Clock (Second Chance)

mcqs.explanation: FIFO is the only common page replacement algorithm that can suffer from Belady's Anomaly. Both OPT and LRU are stack algorithms and do not exhibit this anomaly - they guarantee that increasing frames will not increase page faults. Clock may also exhibit anomalies in some cases.

mcqs.question: The Optimal (OPT) page replacement algorithm is also known as:

options.text: Belady's Optimal Algorithm
MIN (Minimum) Algorithm
Look-ahead Algorithm
Both A and B

mcqs.explanation: The Optimal page replacement algorithm is also called Belady's Optimal Algorithm or MIN (Minimum) algorithm. It selects for eviction the page that will not be used for the longest time in the future, achieving the minimum possible page faults.

mcqs.question: Why is the Optimal (OPT) page replacement algorithm not practical to implement?

options.text: It requires too much memory
It is computationally expensive
It is impossible to predict future page references
It cannot handle dirty pages

mcqs.explanation: The Optimal algorithm requires knowledge of future page references to make the best eviction decision. Since it is impossible to accurately predict a program's future behavior in a practical system, OPT cannot be implemented in real operating systems. It serves only as a theoretical benchmark.

mcqs.question: In the LRU (Least Recently Used) page replacement algorithm, which page is selected for eviction?

options.text: The page that was loaded first into memory
The page that has been accessed the least number of times
The page that was used the longest time ago
The page with the smallest page table entry age

mcqs.explanation: LRU evicts the page that was used the longest time ago (the least recently used page). This is based on the principle of locality - pages used recently are more likely to be used again soon.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is an advantage of LRU over FIFO?

options.text: LRU is easier to implement
LRU does not suffer from Belady's Anomaly
LRU requires less hardware support
LRU always evicts dirty pages first

mcqs.explanation: LRU does not suffer from Belady's Anomaly because it is a stack algorithm. Increasing the number of frames in LRU will never increase the page fault rate. This is a key advantage over FIFO.

mcqs.question: The Clock (Second Chance) algorithm uses which of the following to make replacement decisions?

options.text: A circular linked list of all pages in memory
A reference bit and a circular queue
A counter for each page access
A stack of recently used pages

mcqs.explanation: The Clock (Second Chance) algorithm uses a reference bit (or use bit) and maintains pages in a circular queue (clock hand). When examining a page, if its reference bit is set, the algorithm gives it a second chance and moves on; otherwise, it evicts that page.

mcqs.question: In the Clock algorithm, what happens when a page's reference bit is set (1) when examined?

options.text: The page is immediately evicted
The page is given a second chance and its reference bit is cleared
The algorithm skips to the next page without clearing the bit
The page is marked as a dirty page

mcqs.explanation: When the Clock algorithm examines a page and finds its reference bit set to 1 (meaning it was recently used), it gives the page a second chance by clearing the bit (setting it to 0) and moving to the next page. This allows recently used pages to remain in memory.

mcqs.question: Consider a system with 4 frames and reference string: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 1, 6, 2, 4, 7, 1, 3. How many page faults occur using OPT?
mcqs.explanation: Using OPT with 4 frames: 1,2,3,4 cause 4 faults (frames fill). Page 5 replaces 1 (fault 5) - 1 is not used again. Page 1 replaces 4 (fault 6). Page 6 replaces 3 (fault 7) - 3 is used last among {2,1,6}. Page 2 is already there. Page 4 is already there. Page 7 replaces 6 (fault 8). Page 1 and 3 are already there. Total = 7 faults (wait - let me recalculate). Actually: 1,2,3,4 = 4 faults. 5 replaces 1 = 5th. 1 replaces 2 = 6th. 6 replaces 3 = 7th. Then 2,4 are there. 7 replaces 6 = 8th. 1,3 are there. That's 8 faults.

mcqs.question: What is the main hardware support required for implementing LRU page replacement?

options.text: A simple counter for each page
A stack to track page usage order
Either a counter or a matrix for each page
No hardware support is needed

mcqs.explanation: LRU requires hardware support to track page usage. This can be implemented using either: (1) A counter incremented on each memory access, storing the time of last use for each page, or (2) A matrix method where a page's bit is set when accessed. Both methods require additional hardware complexity.

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of the dirty bit (modified bit) in page replacement?

options.text: To indicate if a page has been accessed recently
To indicate if a page has been modified since being loaded
To indicate if a page is a candidate for eviction
To indicate if a page is currently in the TLB

mcqs.explanation: The dirty bit (or modified bit) indicates whether a page has been modified since it was loaded into memory. If a page is clean (not modified), it can be evicted without writing it back to disk. If dirty, the OS must write the modified page to disk before replacing it, which adds overhead.

mcqs.question: In the context of page replacement, what is a 'page fault'?

options.text: An error in the page table
An attempt to access a page not currently in physical memory
A situation where all frames are occupied
A conflict in the TLB lookup

mcqs.explanation: A page fault occurs when a process attempts to access a page that is not currently in physical memory (not loaded in a frame). The memory management unit (MMU) detects this by checking the valid/invalid bit in the page table, and the OS must bring the required page from secondary storage into memory.

mcqs.question: Which page replacement algorithm approximates LRU and is relatively easy to implement?

options.text: Optimal
FIFO
Clock (Second Chance)
Random

mcqs.explanation: The Clock (Second Chance) algorithm is a practical approximation of LRU that is relatively easy to implement. It uses a reference bit to determine if a page was recently used, similar to LRU's principle, but without the overhead of maintaining exact usage order.

mcqs.question: What is the reference string in the context of page replacement algorithms?

options.text: The sequence of pages in the page table
The chronological sequence of page numbers accessed by a process
The list of pages stored on disk
The mapping between logical and physical pages

mcqs.explanation: The reference string is the chronological sequence of page numbers accessed by a process during its execution. It is used to simulate and analyze the behavior of different page replacement algorithms. For example, for pages 1, 2, 3, 1, 2, the reference string would be: 1,2,3,1,2.

mcqs.question: With 3 frames and reference string A, B, C, D, A, B, E, A, B, C, D, E, what is the number of page faults using LRU?
mcqs.explanation: Using LRU with 3 frames: A,B,C cause 3 faults (frames fill). D replaces A (4th) - A was used 3rd, oldest. A replaces B (5th) - B was used 2nd. B replaces C (6th). E replaces A (7th) - A was used 5th (1,2,3,4,5: A at 5), oldest. A replaces B (8th) - B at 6. B replaces C (9th) - C at 3. C replaces E (10th) - E at 7. D replaces C (11th) - C at 10, oldest. E replaces D (12th). Total = 11 faults.

mcqs.question: What happens when a page selected for eviction has its dirty bit set to 1?

options.text: The page is simply removed from memory
The page must be written back to disk before eviction
The page cannot be evicted until cleared
The page is moved to the swap space automatically

mcqs.explanation: When a page with the dirty bit set (indicating it has been modified) is selected for eviction, the operating system must write (flush) the modified page back to secondary storage (disk) before freeing the frame. This ensures that changes made to the page are preserved.

mcqs.question: Which statement is TRUE about the relationship between LRU and Optimal page replacement?

options.text: LRU always performs better than Optimal
Optimal always performs better than LRU
Optimal is theoretically better but LRU is more practical
There is no relationship between them

mcqs.explanation: The Optimal algorithm achieves the minimum possible page faults theoretically, but it cannot be implemented in practice because it requires knowledge of future page references. LRU is a practical approximation that performs well in most cases and doesn't suffer from Belady's Anomaly, though it requires additional hardware support.
